coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal synthesis of a peony flower-like dual Z-scheme PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic redox activity for organic pollutant under visible-light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Rui
Han, Qiong
Li, Yu
Zhang, Tongqing
Liu, Yi
Zeng, Kunlin
Zhao, Chen
coredata.dc:description: In present study, polyaniline/bismuth bromide/zinc ferrite (PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4) was rationally designed and successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal synthesis method. The crystal structure, chemical composition and morphology of the samples were analyzed by various characterization techniques. The PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite exhibits a higher photocatalytic activity compared with other samples under visible light irradiation. Among all samples, the highest degradation efficiency for RhB over 7% PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 reach 99.26%. Meanwhile, the composite exhibits an excellent catalytic activity for the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB) under visible light, the conversion efficiency can reach 87.1%. Moreover, the ternary composite still maintains a higher photocatalytic activity after three consecutive uses. Additionally, according to PL spectra and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the improved photocatalytic performance of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite can be attributed to the rapid separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs. Finally, the dual Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism suitable for this system was proposed by UV–vis DRS spectra, M-S curves analysis, ESR spectra and free radical capture experiments. This study provides a new research idea for the design and construction of efficient photocatalysts for organic pollutants and other water treatment problems.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal synthesis of a peony flower-like dual Z-scheme PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic redox activity for organic pollutant under visible-light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610500, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite under visible-light exposure and Photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of RhB over different photocatalyst (catalyst dosage: 1g/L).

In present study, polyaniline/bismuth bromide/zinc ferrite (PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4) was rationally designed and successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal synthesis method. The crystal structure, chemical composition and morphology of the samples were analyzed by various characterization techniques. The PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite exhibits a higher photocatalytic activity compared with other samples under visible light irradiation. Among all samples, the highest degradation efficiency for RhB over 7% PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 reach 99.26%. Meanwhile, the composite exhibits an excellent catalytic activity for the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB) under visible light, the conversion efficiency can reach 87.1%. Moreover, the ternary composite still maintains a higher photocatalytic activity after three consecutive uses. Additionally, according to PL spectra and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the improved photocatalytic performance of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite can be attributed to the rapid separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs. Finally, the dual Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism suitable for this system was proposed by UV–vis DRS spectra, M-S curves analysis, ESR spectra and free radical capture experiments. This study provides a new research idea for the design and construction of efficient photocatalysts for organic pollutants and other water treatment problems.

ce:section.ce:para: The removal of organic pollutants exist in sewage discharge can be a challenge and is of great significance to solve environmental pollution and vigorously develop sustainable green chemistry [1,2]. The conversion of solar energy to chemical energy via photocatalytic technology has been regarded as an effective environmental protection strategy to remove organic pollutants. However, the photocatalytic activity is greatly hindered due to the limited light absorption range and low quantum yield for single-phase semiconductor photocatalysts, which cannot meet the needs of practical application [3–7]. Therefore, it is a new and challenging task to explore new type photocatalysts with high efficiency and stability in current photocatalysis studies [8–10].

The activity of catalysts can be improved by fabrication heterojunctions [11], doping [12] and surface morphology modification [13]. Among these methods, the construction of heterojunctions is the most commonly used way to optimize materials, because heterojunctions can realize the spatial separation of electron hole pairs by the interface between two semiconductors with unequal band gaps. According to the previous reports, many composites including two or three semiconductors such as Bi2S3/BiOBr [14], MoS2/g-C3N4/GO [15], BiOBr/RGO/g-C3N4 [16], have achieved great results in the remove pollutants. Recently, Hao [17] and his group successfully prepared a binary catalyst of PANI/BiOBr, and the improvement of photocatalytic properties can be related to photosensitization and inhibition of electron hole recombination. In addition, Liu et al. [18] designed the BiOBr/GO/polyaniline via hydrothermal process, the results show that the presence of GO and polyaniline can significantly expand the spectral response range and improve the quantum yield.

BiOBr, a layered flower structure has a large surface area and high permeability with a moderate energy gap of 2.5 eV, is attracting widespread attentions. What’s more, BiOBr exhibits a higher oxidation ability because its valence band (about 3.19 eV) [19,20] is much higher than that of traditional photocatalysts or oxidants, which can be effectively used for pollutants removal. However, there are still some shortcomings limit the practical application in water treatment, such as the high recombination rate of electrons-holes pairs and the low quantum efficiency. It has been reported that combining other semiconductor with BiOBr is the most effective approach to improve the catalytic activity. Among them, the Z-scheme system shows a higher redox ability compared with the traditional heterojunction, has aroused widespread interest [21–23]. This is because the photo-generated electrons in the CB of photosystem II can recombine with holes in the VB of photosystem I in the Z-scheme system, so that photo-generated electrons with strong reducibility and photo-generated holes with strong oxidation ability can be assembled on the opposite semiconductor surface and participate in photocatalytic reaction [24]. Thus, searching for a suitable photocatalytic material is an effective way to construct a Z-scheme photocatalytic system with BiOBr.

Many researches have shown that introducing conductive polymers with nonlocal π-π conjugated structure can accelerate charge transfer and improve photocatalytic activity [25,26]. Among those, conductive polymer polyaniline (PANI), as a p-type semiconductor material with high carrier mobility and excellent environmental stability, is considered to be one of the most suitable candidate materials. It has been reported that it is widely used in combination with inorganic materials to improve properties, such as PANI/ZnO [27], PANI/Bi12O17Cl2 [28] and PANI/Bi4O5Br2 [29]. What’s more, the band structure can match with BiOBr well, and the potential difference between the VB potential of PANI and the CB potential of BiOBr is 0.32 eV [17]. ZnFe2O4, a typical spinel semiconductor with a typical Eg of approximately 1.9 eV, can absorb up to 653 nm or even larger sunlight. It has attracted more and more attention in photocatalytic field because of its low cost, excellent stability and magnetic recovery. To date, enhanced visible light-driven photocatalytic activities have been observed [30,31] over some composite semiconductors that combine ZnFe2O4 with semiconductors such as ZnFe2O4/AgI [32], ZnFe2O4/ZnO [33,34] and ZnFe2O4/BiVO4 [35]. Therefore, it is a promising method to synthesize recyclable heterojunction composites with a good photocatalytic properties by combining them with photocatalysts.

Motivated by the above analysis, a new type of dual Z-Scheme ternary photocatalyst was designed and synthesized, in which PANI, BiOBr and ZnFe2O4 were used as photosystems I, II and III, respectively. As far as we know, there is no study reporting the preparation and photocatalytic activity of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite. In present study, a series of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composites with different PANI contents were prepared via hydrothermal method. The structure and optical properties were characterized by different characterization methods. The degradation of dyes (RhB) and reduction of colorless organic pollutants (nitrobenzene, NB) under visible-light were used to evaluate the photocatalytic properties of samples. Furthermore, a dual Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism suitable for PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 system was proposed based on the analysis of UV–vis DRS, M-S curves, ESR spectra and energy band theory.

Ferric nitrate nine-hydrate (Fe(NO3)3⋅9H2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O), sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium hydrate (NaOH) were purchased from Kelong chemical reagent factory (Chengdu, China). Polyaniline (PANI) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and the experimental water was deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4 by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, 10 mmol Fe(NO3)3⋅9H2O and 5 mmol Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O were added to 30 ml deionized water and stirred vigorously for 20 min at room temperature. Then, adjusting the pH of solution to 13 with 6 mol/L NaOH solution and continuing stir for an hour. After that, the mixed solution was transferred to the 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 13 h [36]. After cooling to ambient temperature, the red-brown ZnFe2O4 precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed alternately with pure water and absolute ethanol for three times. Finally, the precipitates were dried at 70 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven.

The PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst was prepared by a solvothermal method as illustrated in the Scheme 1 . Firstly, a certain amount of PANI and ZnFe2O4 were dispersed in 20 ml deionized water by sonication for 20 min. The mass fraction of ZnFe2O4 in the weight of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 was controlled to be 7% in this study. Subsequently, the 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O was added into the above dispersion and stirred for another 20 min. Then, 20 ml aqueous solution containing 1 mmol NaBr was added slowly and maintained stirring for 30 min. After that, the suspension was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, keep it at 150 °C for 24 h [17]. And then cool at room temperature naturally, the precipitation was centrifuged and washed for three times with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried for 24 h at 60 °C to obtain the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite. The obtained PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 were named as x% PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 (x = 3, 5, 7 and 9, which was the mass fraction of PANI in the weight of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4). For comparison, pure BiOBr, the BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 and BiOBr/PANI composite were prepared in term of a similar procedure without adding PANI and ZnFe2O4, without adding PANI and without adding ZnFe2O4, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the photocatalysts were determined by X-ray diffractometer (X’Pert PRO MRD, Panako, Netherlands) with diffraction angle 2θ = 5 − 80° by using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out with a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher Scientific). The surface morphology and structure of the samples were observed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, ZEISS EV0 MA15, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Joel-2100F, Japan). The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) in the range of 200–800 nm were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 850, America). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the catalysts were investigated by Fluorescence spectrometer (PerKinEImer LS55, America) with an emission wavelength at 325 nm.

The photocatalytic activities were evaluated by the degradation of RhB (20 mg/L) and reduction of NB (5 mg/L), and a high pressure mercury lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source. The 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed into 50 ml RhB or NB solution by ultrasonic. Subsequently, the suspension was stirred in darkness for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium before illumination. The 3 ml suspension was centrifuged at intervals of 5 min during irradiation and the supernatant was determined using a Hitachi UV1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The maximum absorption wavelengths of RhB, NB, MB, MO, NFX and BPA are 554 nm, 267 nm, 664 nm, 464 nm, 278 nm and 276 nm, respectively.

The photo-electrochemical properties of the samples were measured by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CHI Co., China). A Pt electrode, standard calomel electrode and FTO electrode are used as opposite electrode, reference electrode and working electrode respectively. Firstly, 10 mg photocatalyst and 200 μl 5% naphthol solution were added to 600 μl absolute ethanol and sonicated for 20 min to form a uniform dispersion. The dispersion was dropped onto the conductive surface of FTO glass electrode and ventilated as well as dried overnight at room temperature. A 200 W high pressure mercury lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the light source, and the electrolyte solution was 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The transient photocurrent response spectra of the samples were obtained via several on-off cycles at 0 V voltage. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of the sample were obtained via applying an AC voltage of 10 mV to the frequency range of 105 Hz-10−2 Hz. Then, the flat-band potential (Ufb) of photocatalyst was determined by Mott-Sckottky (M-S) method, and the Ufb values were obtained via scanning the electrode potential from −1.5 to 1.0 V at the frequency of 1000 Hz and velocity 0.01 V/s in dark.

The XRD spectra provide the crystal structure and phase composition of the photocatalyst. Fig. 1 is the XRD patterns of PANI, ZnFe2O4, BiOBr, BiOBr/PANI, BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4. Pure PANI is amorphous, which attributed to its perpendicularity and periodicity parallel to the polymer chains of PANI [17]. For pure ZnFe2O4, the diffraction peaks at 29.75°, 35.11°, 42.71°, 53.10°, 56.51°, 62.10° are corresponded to (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0) diffraction planes respectively, which can be readily indexed to the spinel phase of ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS 22-1012) [37]. The different diffraction peaks of BiOBr located at 2θ = 10.82°, 21.91°, 25.22°, 31.84°, 32.17°, 33.21°, 39.5°, 44.84°, 50.63°, 56.25°, 57.24°, 69.61°and 76.63°, are indexed to the (0 0 1), (0 0 2), (0 1 1), (0 1 2), (0 0 4), (0 0 3), (1 1 2), (0 1 4), (1 1 4), (2 1 2), (2 1 1) and (3 1 0) diffraction surfaces of the tetragonal phase (JCPDS 09-0393) [38]. According to XRD analysis results, it can be concluded that there is no effect on the lattice structure of BiOBr at the existence of PANI. No obvious PANI diffraction peaks are observed in BiOBr/PANI and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite, which can be attribute to the low content of PANI and the weak crystallinity. Furthermore, some characteristic peaks of ZnFe2O4 (marked as ★ in XRD pattern) are observed in the XRD spectra of BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite. The results indicate that ZnFe2O4 has been successfully loaded on the surface and internal of BiOBr and BiOBr/PANI, and no other peaks with impurities appeared shows that the photocatalyst has good crystallinity. Then, the molar ratio of Zn/Fe was determined with a certain concentration of sample solution as shown in Table 1 . The results show that the molar ratio of Zn/Fe in ZnFe2O4 and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite is approximately 1:2, which indicates that the corrosion degree of Zn and Fe in the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite is small.

The surface morphology and microstructures of the samples were measured by SEM. Fig. 3 a clearly reveals the three-dimensional flower-like structure of BiOBr, which is assembled by ultra-thin nanosheets from different angles. The nanoparticles are released from the flower core, which can form a large number of free open spaces in the flower, enhancing the light absorption greatly. As shown in Fig. 3b, the morphology of PANI is mainly nanofibers with a diameter of approximately 75 nm and show agglomeration. For the case of PANI/BiOBr (Fig. 3c), PANI displays a morphology of the irregularity nanoplates with a thickness of about 40 nm. It was observed that ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles are dispersed on the surface and in the internal structure of BiOBr after introducing ZnFe2O4. From the SEM images of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite (Fig. 3f), it can be seen that ZnFe2O4 tightly immobilized on the surface of BiOBr flower-like structure wrapped by PANI. Therefore, the PANI-BiOBr-ZnFe2O4 structure with a close and large contact surface is formed. It can not only accelerate the separation and migration of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, but also provide more reaction sites conducive to photocatalytic reaction. Briefly, SEM images show that PANI, ZnFe2O4 and BiOBr particles are well blended, thus ensuring the formation of high quality tunnel junctions. In addition, the EDS spectra as shown in Fig. 3g confirms that the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite are composed of Br, Bi, Zn, Fe, N and O elements. Furthermore, the element mapping is used to analyze the spatial distribution of elements. As presented in Fig. 3h, it further shows that the components are uniformly and densely distributed on the whole surface of the composite.

The TEM and HRTEM images of the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite are used to confirm the more detailed structure and morphology of the composite and displayed in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from Fig. 4a and b, the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite synthesized via the hydrothermal method has a three-dimensional spatial layered microsphere structure, consisting of a large number of petal-shaped two-dimensional nanosheets with gaps in the middle. ZnFe2O4 particles are uniformly coated on the surface or inside of BiOBr and a layer of PANI is coated on the edge of BiOBr layered microspheres. The solitary pair electrons on the outer nitrogen atom of PANI can easily adsorb pollutant molecules and enhance photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, two different lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.277 nm and 0.255 nm are observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 4c). The fringe interval of 0.277 nm is in line with the (1 0 2) crystal plane for tetragonal-phase BiOBr [38], whereas the fringe intervals of 0.255 nm can be assigned to the (3 1 1) crystal plane of tetragonal-phase ZnFe2O4 [37]. Moreover, the tightly interfacial crosslinking structures of BiOBr, ZnFe2O4 and PANI accelerating the separation and transfer of electron-holes pairs can be clearly observed from HRTEM images (Fig. 4d).

In this study, the optical absorption properties and band structure of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy. Fig. 5 a and 5b exhibit the DRS spectra of PANI, BiOBr, ZnFe2O4, BiOBr/ZnFe2O4, BiOBr/PANI, PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 and PANI, which reflect their optical properties in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Obviously, the optical absorption edge of BiOBr is at about 480 nm. ZnFe2O4 displays an extensive absorption in the whole visible light region. In addition, PANI also shows a strong absorption under visible light. The PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite exhibits light absorption in almost the whole visible-light spectrum, indicating that the formation of heterojunctions greatly broadens the visible light response range. Moreover, the band gap energy (Eg) values of BiOBr, PANI and ZnFe2O4 can be calculated according to Kubelka-Munk function with the following formula (1).
(1)
α h v = A
hv -
E g
n / 2
where α, h, v, Eg and A are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, optical frequency, band gap and constant, respectively [43,44]. Since BiOBr and PANI are indirect bandgap semiconductors, n is 4 [19,25], while for direct bandgap semiconductor ZnFe2O4, n takes 1 [11]. Thus, the Eg values are calculated by intersecting the tangent and abscissa at the inflection point according to the plots of (αhv)1/2 and (αhv)2 versus energy (hv), as presented in Fig. 5c-e, and they are 2.78 eV, 1.97 eV and 1.89 eV for BiOBr, PANI and ZnFe2O4, respectively.

Furthermore, in order to evaluate the wide applicability of the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst for water treatment and eliminate the influence of dyes on photocatalytic activity of catalysts, methyl orange (MO: 10 mg/L), methyl blue (MB: 20 mg/L) and colorless pollutants such as bisphenol A (BPA: 5 mg/L), norfloxacin (NFX: 5 mg/L) were degraded under the same degradation conditions. As shown in Figure S1, after visible light irradiation for 60 min, the removal rates are 95.32%, 90.97%, 75.64% and 89.75% for MO, MB, BPA and NFX, respectively. The experimental results indicate that PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite can be widely used in the field of organic wastewater degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of operational parameters on RhB dye degradation

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of photocatalyst dosage (0.25–1.5 g/L) on the degradation of RhB dye (20 mg/L) by PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite was studied under the visible light irradiation for 30 min. As shown in Figure S2a, the degradation efficiency of RhB dyes increases significantly with the increase of catalyst dosage (<1 g/L). Subsequently, the degradation rate of RhB slightly decreases when the photocatalyst dosage increased from 1 g/L to 1.5 g/L. It can be attributed to the shielding effect and reduced light penetration caused by the increased turbidity of the suspension, which limited the effective light absorption of the photocatalyst, and thus the photocatalytic activity decreases. Considering the photolysis efficiency and cost, the optimum amount of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 dosage is 1 g/L for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration of RhB dye
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of initial dye concentration on the photocatalytic activity of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite (1 g/L) for RhB degradation under visible light irradiation for 30 min was investigated. As illustrated in Figure S2b, RhB dye could be almost completely degraded at the initial concentration of 10 and 20 mg/L, and when the initial dye concentration increases to 25 mg/L, the degradation efficiency of RhB still reaches to approximately 85%. This indicates that PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite has a better photodegradation efficiency in a wide range of RhB dye concentration. However, the photocatalytic activity of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite decreases significantly when the dye concentration is further increased to 30 mg/L. This decline can be attributed to insufficient active components provided by the catalyst and competition between excessive dye molecules, leading to the reduction of photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst.

The degradation rate of RhB dye (20 mg/L) at differently initial pH values was studied using PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst (1 g/L) under visible light for 30 min. The results is shown in Figure S2c, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite in different pH values for RhB dye solution is significantly different. The PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity in near-neutral pH solution (pH = 7) and a better degradation efficiency in the alkaline solution (pH = 10). However, the photocatalytic activity of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite in the acidic solution decreases significantly (pH = 4). In the alkaline solution, the surface of BiOBr is negatively charged, and RhB cationic dye molecules are easily adsorbed on the surface of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O 4 composite photocatalyst, which promotes the increased contact between dye molecules and the active sites of the catalyst as well as the dyes degradation. On the contrary, in the acidic solution, the surface of BiOBr is positively charged, and the adsorption of RBB dye molecules on the catalyst surface decreases because of coulomb repulsion, thus the photocatalytic efficiency becomes worse.

The stability and reusability of photocatalyst is an important factor in practical application. The photocatalytic experiments of BiOBr and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 were carried out for three times under the same conditions to investigate the stability of the sample. Fig. 8 a shows that the degradation rate of pure BiOBr decreased from 60.25% to 50.26% under visible light irradiation. However, the photocatalytic properties of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite for RhB degradation did not decreased significantly. Moreover, the degradation rate of RhB decreased slightly even after three consecutive uses, only from 99.26% to 97.86%, indicating the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite displays a higher stability in RhB degradation. XRD pattern is used to measure the structural changes of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 after cyclic experiments and as shown in Fig. 8b. There is no obvious different peak appeared in the XRD pattern of the 3 times used PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite, which indicates the structure of the photocatalyst is extremely stable and have not been significantly changed during the reaction process. Fig. 8c exhibits the TEM of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite after 3 reuses. It can be seen that the composite still keeps a good petal-shape after three reuses. ZnFe2O4 particles are uniformly dispersed on the surface or inside of BiOBr, and a layer of PANI is coated on the edge of the BiOBr microspheres. Moreover, no other obvious lattice fringes for impurity is observed in the HRTEM image of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite after 3 reuses (Fig. 8d). In addition, PANI plays a vital role in this system and previous studies have shown that the stability of PANI affects the photocatalytic activity of the whole photocatalytic system. We used the XPS spectrum of N 1 s to describe the stability of PANI after three reuses. As presented in Fig. 8f, there is no significant difference compared with the fresh PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 although the strength and location of some functional groups of PANI have changed, which means that PANI exhibits a good stability in photocatalytic process.

The fluorescence photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy of the samples were carried out to investigate the separation and recombination of photo-generated carriers. As shown in Figure S3a, the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite exhibits the lowest PL intensity, which demonstrates a higher separation efficiency for photogenerated electron hole pairs, and this is beneficial to improving photocatalytic performance [45]. Similarly, the results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots also provide evidence of visible-light photocatalytic activity, and the arc radius is inversely proportional to their photocatalytic activity. It can be seen from Figure S2b that the arc radius of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 on EIS Nyquist diagram is the smallest compared with BiOBr, BiOBr/PANI and BiOBr/ZnFe2O4. Generally, a smaller arc radius indicates a lower charge transfer resistance and a higher charge transfer efficiency [46]. Thus, the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 exhibits a higher charge transfer efficiency, which promotes the photocatalytic activity.

The free radical capture experiments were carried out to investigate the main active species in the system during the photo-degradation process [47,48]. The isopropanol (IPA, 10 mmol/L), para-benzoquinone (BQ, 10 mmol/L) and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na, 1 mmol/L) were added into the reaction system, which acted as scavengers for capturing hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide radicals (
O2 −) and photogenerated holes (h+) [49–51], respectively. As presented in Fig. 9 a, when EDTA-2Na and BQ were added to the system, the degradation rate of RhB decreased sharply from 99.26% to 63.75% and 24.68%, respectively. However, when IPA was added, the degradation rate of RhB has a slightly decrease from 99.26% to 98.21%. Hence, it can be seen that the
O2 − and h+ formed in the photocatalytic system are the main active species to improve the photocatalytic activity.

The ESR spin-trap with DMPO technique was measured under visible light irradiation to further determine the main active species of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 in photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 9b, after 3 min visible light irradiation, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − appeared, and the peak intensities gradually increased with the increase of irradiation time, while no signal of DMPO-
O2 − for BiOBr and PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 are detected in dark. Moreover, the signal intensity of DMPO-
O2 − of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite is obviously stronger than that of BiOBr, indicating that PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite can produces more
O2 − free radicals due to the double Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism, which is beneficial to improve photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH are observed in Fig. 9c, while the signals of
OH are relatively weak. The results show that
OH plays a minor role in photocatalytic reaction. The ESR results are in good agreement with the free radical trapping experiments.

In addition, the flat-band potential (Ufb) values of BiOBr, PANI and ZnFe2O4 are calculated using the horizontal axis intercept of the Mott-Schottky plots [52]. As presented in Fig. 10 a, the negative slope of M-S plot for BiOBr indicates that it is a p-type semiconductor. The Ufb of BiOBr is 2.84 V (vs SCE), so the VB of BiOBr is 2.84 V (vs SCE, VB≈Ufb) [53]. Then, the value of VB is 3.08 V vs the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE, NHE = SCE + 0.24 V) [54]. For PANI and ZnFe2O4, the positive slope of the M-S plots (Fig. 10b and 10c) represent that they are n-type semiconductors. The Ufb of PANI and ZnFe2O4 are −0.53 V and −0.94 V (vs SCE), respectively. Then, the values of CB are equal to −0.73 V and −1.14 V (vs SCE, CB≈Ufb-0.2 V) [55]. Thus, the CB values of PANI and ZnFe2O4 are determined to be −0.49 V and −0.90 V vs the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE, NHE = SCE + 0.24 V). The relative positions of the conduction bands (CB) of BiOBr and the valence bands (VB) of PANI and ZnFe2O4 are respectively derived from the Eq. Eg = EVB-ECB . Therefore, the conduction band potentials (ECB) value of BiOBr is 0.30 V vs NHE and the valence band potentials (EVB) values of PANI and ZnFe2O4 are 1.48 V and 0.99 V vs NHE, respectively. And Fig. 10d displays the approximate energy band diagram of BiOBr, PANI and ZnFe2O4.

On the basic of the results discussed above, a possible charge transfer mechanism of PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 for the RhB photodegradation was proposed. First of all, BiOBr, PANI and ZnFe2O4 can absorb visible light and generate the electron-hole pairs. If electron transfer follows the traditional heterojunction type, the photo-generated electrons in conduction bands (CB) of PANI and ZnFe2O4 will transfer to the CB of BiOBr, resulting in photo-generated electrons and holes accumulating in CB of BiOBr and VB of PANI and ZnFe2O4, respectively. It is noteworthy that the CB potential of BiOBr (0.30 V vs NHE) is positive than the redox potential of
O2 − (E (O2/
O2 −) = −0.33 V vs NHE) [56], thus, the accumulated photoelectrons can hardly reduce O2 to
O2 −. Similarly, the photogenic holes in the VB of PANI (1.48 V vs NHE) and ZnFe2O4 (0.99 V vs NHE) cannot oxidize H2O/OH− to
OH (E (H2O/
OH) = +2.40 V vs NHE) [57], which is inconsistent with the results of free radical capture experiments. As a consequence, a dual Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism is proposed and presented in Fig. 11 . According to the double Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism, photo-generated electrons in CB of BiOBr will migrate to VB of PANI and ZnFe2O4 and recombine with holes. As a result, photo-generated electrons with stronger reductive ability accumulate in the CB of PANI (−0.49 V vs NHE) and ZnFe2O4 (−0.90 V vs NHE), and the electrons can easily reduce O2 to
O2 −. Meanwhile, the photo-generated holes accumulated in VB of BiOBr (3.08 V vs NHE) can not only oxidize pollutants directly, but also oxidize H2O/OH− to
OH. This is consistent with the experimental results of free radical capture, which confirms that h+ and
O2 − are the main active species in the photocatalytic process. The pollutants react with
O2 − and h+ formed in the photocatalytic process and decompose into inorganic small molecules gradually. The mechanism of the double Z-scheme photocatalytic degradation can be summarized as follows:
PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 + hv → e − (PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4) + h+ (PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4)
e − (BiOBr) + h+ (PANI) → BiOBr + PANI
e − (BiOBr) + h+ (ZnFe2O4) → BiOBr + ZnFe2O4
e − (PANI) + e − (ZnFe2O4) + O2 → PANI + ZnFe2O4 +
O2 −
O2 + 2H+ + 2e − → H2O2 (0.68 V vs. NHE)
h+ (BiOBr) + H2O2 → BiOBr + O2 + 2H+
(
O2 −, h+ (BiOBr)) + Pollutants → degradation products

To further study the degradation intermediates, the oxidation products produced in the photocatalytic process were determined by MS. Figure S4 exhibits a mass spectrogram of RhB and its degradation products after illumination for 0, 15 min and 30 min, respectively. In this photocatalyst system, the strong peak at m/z 443.2 is the ion peak of RhB molecules, and the presence of three products with m/z 415.2, 387.2 and 359.2 are the ion peak of RhB molecule after removing one, two and three ethyl groups respectively. The peak at m/z 491.3 originates from the intermediate products formed by hydroxylation of RhB molecules via three
OH. After visible light irradiation for 30 min, the new peak at m/z 318.3 can be attributed to the intermediate products of RhB formed by the hydroxylation of two diethylamine fragments and hydroxylation of one
OH, while the peak at 274.2 is derived from the substance after the decarboxylation of the intermediate products. The peaks at 230 and 126 are caused by
OH attacking the single bond connecting the upper and lower conjugated structures in RhB molecules, and the rest of the peaks in Figure S4c are ion peaks and fragment peaks of substances after RhB molecules degraded into small molecules. According to the experimental data of MS, the possible photodegradation pathways are exhibited in Fig. 12 . The degradation process of RhB molecule can be attributed to the following two processes: the deethylation process removes ethyl, carboxyl and ethylamine groups from the periphery of RhB molecule structure. The hydroxylation process destroys the conjugated structure of RhB molecule and forms small molecules, then mineralizes into CO2 and H2O. It is worth noting that the deethylation and hydroxylation process are simultaneously involved in the degradation of RhB.

A novel dual Z-scheme photocatalyst PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 was successfully designed and synthesized via a hydro-thermal method. The photocatalyst exhibits an excellent electron transfer ability and structural characteristic, and the photocatalytic performance is greatly enhanced by the separation of electrons-holes pairs via the synergistic effect of heterojunction. Thus, the prepared PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 photocatalyst exhibits a higher bifunctional photocatalytic properties for the degradation of RhB and the reduction of NB compared with other catalysts. Especially for 7% PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4, its degradation rate and catalytic reduction rate is 99.26% and 87.1%, respectively. Additionally, the results of cyclic experiments also indicate the stability and reusability of the PANI/BiOBr/ZnFe2O4 composite. The establishment of this double Z-scheme photocatalytic system will provide a new idea for the development of visible light response composite material for solving environmental problems.
